title.en: Expressing Hurt Feelings: "Actually, I felt a bit hurt."
title.ko: 사실 나 좀 서운했어 (친구에게 서운함 표현하기)

description.en: Feeling neglected or disappointed? Learn how to use the "magic word" 서운하다 to express your feelings softly without starting a fight.
description.ko: 친구에게 화를 내기엔 애매하지만 내심 속상할 때! 관계를 해치지 않고 내 감정을 솔직하고 정중하게 전달하는 리액션을 배워봅니다.

tags: Situation. Social. Friendship. Emotion. Conflict. Soft Speech. Season 2

content.speaker: John
content.text: Mina, something happened with a friend recently. He went to a concert we both liked with someone else, without even asking me.

content.speaker: John
content.text: I’m not exactly "angry," but I feel a bit sad and neglected. In English, I'd say "I'm disappointed," but is there a more "Korean" way to say this?

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 그 기분 뭔지 알 것 같아요! 그럴 땐 화가 난다고 하기엔 너무 무겁고 그냥 넘어가기엔 속상하죠.
content.translation: Ah, I know that feeling! In that case, saying "I'm angry" is too heavy, but just letting it go hurts.

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 한국인들은 이럴 때..., "서운하다" 라는 단어를 정말 많이 써요.
content.translation: Koreans use the word "Seoun-hada" really often in these situations.

content.speaker: John
content.text: "서운하다"? Is it different from being "disappointed"?

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 비슷하지만 조금 더 애정이 섞인 표현이에요. "난 널 좋아하는데 넌 나한테 왜 그랬어?" 같은 느낌이죠.
content.translation: It's similar, but it's an expression mixed with more 'affection.' It's like, "I like you, so why did you do that to me?"

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 오늘은 솔직하게 내 마음을 전하면서 관계도 지키는 서운함의 리액션들을 배워볼게요!
content.translation: Today, we'll learn 'reactions of hurt feelings' that convey your heart honestly while protecting the relationship!

content.speaker: John
content.text: 사실 나 좀 서운했어.

content.speaker: John
content.text: By saying "Sasil" at the beginning, it feels like I'm opening up my heart.

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 맞아요! "사실" 은..., "Actually" 라는 뜻인데 진지한 대화를 시작할 때 아주 좋은 완충제가 돼요.
content.translation: Right! "Sasil" means "Actually," and it acts as a very good buffer when starting a serious conversation.

insight.title: 사실 나 좀 서운했어
insight.usage_tip: 사실(Actually) + 나(I) + 좀(A bit) + 서운했어(Was hurt/disappointed). Unlike '화나다'(to be angry), '서운하다' focuses on your feelings rather than blaming the other person.

content.speaker: John
content.text: What if I want to acknowledge their side first?

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 그럴 땐..., "바쁜 건 알지만 그래도 서운하더라" 라고 말해보세요.
content.translation: In that case, try saying "I know you're busy, but I still felt hurt."

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 상대방의 상황을 이해한다고 먼저 말해주면 대화가 훨씬 부드러워지거든요.
content.translation: The conversation becomes much smoother if you say you understand the other person's situation first.

insight.title: 바쁜 건 알지만
insight.usage_tip: Using this "buffer phrase" prevents the other person from getting defensive.

content.speaker: John
content.text: Okay, let's jump in. Mina and I planned to study together, but she went to a cafe with another friend without telling me. I’m feeling a bit "seoun-hae."

content.speaker: John
content.text: 미나씨 저기... 혹시 어제 친구들이랑 카페 갔었어요?
content.translation: Mina, um... did you go to a cafe with friends yesterday by any chance?

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 아! 네! 갑자기 연락이 와서 잠깐 다녀왔어요. 왜요?
content.translation: Ah, yes! They called suddenly, so I went for a bit. Why?

content.speaker: John
content.text: 아... 같이 공부하기로 했었는데 연락이 없어서요. 사실 나 좀 서운했어.
content.translation: Ah... we were supposed to study together, and since I didn't hear from you... Actually, I was a bit hurt.

insight.title: 사실 나 좀 서운했어
insight.usage_tip: John isn't shouting; he's sharing his feelings. This makes Mina want to apologize rather than argue.

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 어머! 존! 미안해요. 제가 정신이 없어서 연락하는 걸 깜빡했어요. 진짜 미안해요.
content.translation: Oh my, John! I'm sorry. I was so hectic that I forgot to contact you. I'm truly sorry.

content.speaker: John
content.text: 말해줘서 고마워요. 다음에는 미리 말해줄 수 있어요?
content.translation: Thank you for telling me. Could you let me know in advance next time?

insight.title: 말해줘서 고마워요
insight.usage_tip: This is a great way to wrap up the conversation and move forward.

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 그럼요! 다음에는 제가 맛있는 커피 살게요. 미안해요!
content.translation: Of course! Next time, the delicious coffee is on me. I'm sorry!

content.speaker: John
content.text: Wow, using "서운하다" really changed the vibe. Instead of an argument, it became a heart-to-heart talk!

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 맞아요. 한국어에서 서운함은 서로를 아끼는 마음이 베이스에 깔려 있다는 걸 기억하세요.
content.translation: Right. Remember that in Korean, 'seoun-ham' is based on 'cherishing each other.'

content.speaker: John
content.text: Let's review our "gentle hurt" reactions one last time.

content.speaker: John
content.text: 사실 나 좀 서운했어!
content.translation: Actually, I was a bit hurt!

content.speaker: John
content.text: 바쁜 건 알지만 그래도 서운하더라!
content.translation: I know you're busy, but I still felt hurt!

content.speaker: John
content.text: 내 연락 기다린 줄 알았는데 서운해!
content.translation: I thought you were waiting for my call, so I'm hurt!

content.speaker: John
content.text: 말해줘서 고마워!
content.translation: Thank you for telling me!

content.speaker: John
content.text: 다음부터는 미리 말해줘!
content.translation: Please tell me in advance from next time!

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 여러분도 소중한 사람에게 마음이 상했다면, 꾹 참지 말고 정중하게 리액션해 보세요.
content.translation: Everyone, if your feelings are hurt by someone precious, don't just hold it in; try reacting politely.

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: "사실 나 좀 서운했어."
content.translation: Actually, I was a bit hurt.

content.speaker: John
content.text: That's all for today. See you in the next episode.

content.speaker: Mina
content.text: 다음에 또 만나요. 안녕~!
content.translation: See you again next time. Bye~!

vocabulary.meaning: To be hurt / To be disappointed / To feel neglected
vocabulary.example: 친구가 연락을 안 해서 조금 서운했어요. (I was a bit hurt because my friend didn't contact me.)
사실은 제가 그 일을 깜빡하고 안 했어요. (In fact, I forgot and didn't do that work.)
그녀는 강아지에게 아주 큰 애정을 가지고 있어요. (She has great affection for her puppy.)

vocabulary.meaning: To be hectic / Out of one's mind
vocabulary.example: 오늘 아침엔 너무 정신이 없어서 아침을 못 먹었어요. (I was so hectic this morning that I couldn't eat breakfast.)

vocabulary.meaning: To be upset / To feel distressed
vocabulary.example: 시험 결과가 좋지 않아서 정말 속상해요. (I'm really upset because the exam results weren't good.)
친구 사이에도 지켜야 할 선이 있어요. (There are lines that should be kept even between friends.)
